elements.sent-2411-002: We introduce a new class of computationally tractable scattering problems in unbounded domains, which we call decomposable problems.
elements.sent-2411-003: In these decomposable problems, the computational domain can be split into a finite collection of subdomains in which the scatterer has a ``simple" structure.
elements.sent-2411-004: A subdomain is simple if the domain Green's function for this subdomain is either available analytically or can be computed numerically with arbitrary accuracy by a tractable method.
elements.sent-2411-005: These domain Green's functions are then used to reformulate the scattering problem as a system of boundary integral equations on the union of the subdomain boundaries.
elements.sent-2411-006: This reformulation gives a practical numerical method, as the resulting integral equations can then be solved, to any desired degree of accuracy, by using coordinate complexification over a finite interval, and standard discretization techniques.
elements.sent-2411-007: There are many circumstances where one would like to examine how waves scatter off of unbounded interfaces.
elements.sent-2411-008: In this paper we consider only scalar waves described as perturbations of the Helmholtz equation.
elements.sent-2411-009: A typical unbounded scatterer is a network of open wave-guides described by $\Delta+q({\bx )+k^2$ where the potential is of the form \begin{equation}\label{eqn1} q({\bx})=q_0({\bx})+\sum_{j=1}^Nq_j({\bx}-\langle {\bx},\omega_j\rangle \omega_j) \chi_{[1,\infty)}(r_j\langle {\bx},\omega_j\rangle).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Here $q_0$ is a compactly supported function, the $\{q_j:\:j=1,N\}$ are compactly supported functions of one fewer variable, the $\{\omega_j\}$ are distinct unit vectors, and the $\{r_j\}$ are positive numbers.

elements.sent-2411-161: Due to the presence of wave-guide modes, the solutions~$\rho$ and~$\mu$ do not decay as~$x_1\to \pm \infty$.

elements.sent-2411-163: We therefore use the coordinate complexification method introduced in the introduction.
elements.sent-2411-164: This is quite similar to the approach taken in~\cite{BBD_SIAM_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-165: In this method, we deform the boundary~$\gamma$ to the curve \begin{equation}\tilde\gamma = \{(x_1+i \psi(x_1),\pm d)\; |\; x_1\in \bbR \},\end{equation} where~$\psi$ is a monotonically increasing function supported on~$[-L,L]^C$, which goes to~$\pm\infty$ as~$x_1\to\pm\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-166: The choice of~$L$ determines the strip of~$\bbR^2$ on which we can recover the solution~$u$.
elements.sent-2411-167: We choose the normal to~$\tilde\gamma$ to be~$\bs n= (0,\operatorname{sign (x_2))$, which agrees with the normal to~$\gamma$ where they overlap.}
elements.sent-2411-168: The integral operators on~$\tilde\gamma$ are the same as those on~$\gamma$, except that they are based on the analytic continuation of the Green's function: \begin{equation}\tilde G_k(\bx;\by) := \frac{i}4H_0^{(1)}(k r(\bx;\by)),\end{equation} where \begin{equation}r(\bx;\by) := \sqrt{(x_1-y_1)^2+(x_2-y_2)^2}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-169: Choosing~$\psi$ to be non-decreasing ensures that \begin{equation}\Im[(x_1-y_1+i(\psi(x_1)-\psi(y_1))^2+(x_2-y_2)^2]\geq 0,\end{equation} for all~$x_1,y_1,x_2,y_2\in\bbR$.
elements.sent-2411-170: Therefore $r(\cdot;\by)|_{\tilde \gamma}$ does not cross the branch cut of the square root.
elements.sent-2411-171: The advantage of this contour deformation comes from the fact that our kernels and densities are asymptotic to linear combinations of functions of the form \begin{equation}e^{i\eta \sqrt{x_1^2}} (x_1^2)^{\alpha/2}\end{equation} for various choices of~$\eta$ and~$\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-172: When functions of this form are analytically continued to~$\tilde\gamma$, they are bounded by the exponentially decaying functions \begin{equation}C e^{- \eta |\Im x_1|}|x_1|^\alpha.\label{eq:exp_deacy}\end{equation} This exponential decay allows us to truncate~$\tilde\gamma$ when~$|\Im x_1|$ is large enough.
elements.sent-2411-173: We now discuss a numerical method for solving the integral equation~\eqref{eq:wave_guide_IE on the contour~$\tilde \gamma$.}
elements.sent-2411-174: For the remainder of this paper, we take the complexification function to defined by $\psi(x_1) = 20 \operatorname{erfc ( (x_1+L+30)/5)-\operatorname{erfc ( -(x_1-L-30)/5)$, which is smooth and zero to machine precision on~$[-L,L]$.}}

elements.section-2411-006: The scattering matrix and radiated power
elements.sent-2411-368: In the design process, it is often important to know how much power is transferred through a junction, how much is reflected, and how much is lost to radiation.
elements.sent-2411-369: This information is encoded in the scattering matrix.
elements.sent-2411-370: With the ability to compute scattering matrices, we can build a very efficient simulation of an optical circuit.
elements.sent-2411-371: In this section, we describe how to build the scattering matrix and how to use it to measure the radiated power.
elements.sent-2411-372: As described above, the wave-guide modes are of the form \begin{equation}u_{j;l,r,\pm} (x_1,x_2) = e^{\pm i\xi_{j;l,r}} v_{j;l,r}(x_2).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-373: The~$\xi_{j;l,r}$'s are the roots of~\eqref{eq:mode_eqn that lie in the interior of~$(k,k_{1;l,r )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-374: To find these roots, we use the smoother equivalent form of equation~\eqref{eq:mode_eqn \begin{equation}\label{eq:smth_mode} f_{l,r}(\xi) = \lp k_{1;l,r}^2+k^2-2\xi^2\rp \sin\lp 2d\sqrt{k_{1;l,r}^2 - \xi^2} \rp - 2\sqrt{\xi^2-k^2}\sqrt{k_{1;l,r}^2 - \xi^2}\cos\lp2d\sqrt{k_{1;l,r}^2 - \xi^2} \rp=0.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-375: Not that while $k_{1;l,r}$ is always a root of this equation it is never a root of the Wronskian.
elements.sent-2411-376: To find the roots of~$f_{l,r}$, we build a 500 term Chebyshev expansion of each function.
elements.sent-2411-377: To avoid a loss of accuracy caused by the square root singularities, we build this expansion on the interval~$(k+0.01,k_{1;l,r -0.01)$, which allows us to find all of the modes for most wave-guides.}
elements.sent-2411-378: Wave-guides with modes outside this interval could be handled with a longer expansion over a bigger interval.
elements.sent-2411-379: We find the roots of those expansions using their colleague matrices (see~\cite{good1961colleague ).}
elements.sent-2411-380: Any roots of those expansions that lie in~$(k+0.01,k_{1;l,r -0.01)$ are the frequencies of the wave-guide modes.}
elements.sent-2411-381: Now that we have found these modes, we can compute the projection of~$u$ onto them.
elements.sent-2411-382: The projection onto~$u_{j;r,+}$ is defined to be \begin{equation}c^r_j = \int_{-\infty}^\infty \overline{u_{j;r,+}}(x_1,x_2) u(x_1,x_2) dx_2 \label{eq:proj_def}\end{equation} for any~$x_1>0$.
elements.sent-2411-383: The projections onto~$u_{j;l,-}$ are defined similarly.
elements.sent-2411-384: By equation (206) in~\cite{EpWG2023_1 , these coefficients can also be computed by the formula \begin{equation}c^r_j = \int_{-\infty}^\infty v_{r,j}(x_2) \lp -i\xi^r_j \sigma + \tau \rp dx_2.\label{eq:proj_form}\end{equation} As further verification of our solver, we compute the projections of~$u$ in~\figref{fig:match_soln using both formulas.}}
elements.sent-2411-385: We discretize~\eqref{eq:proj_def using~$x_1=5$ and~$x_1=25$ and eight 16th order Gauss-Legendre panels on the numerical support of the wave-guide modes.}
elements.sent-2411-386: The the difference in each of the computed coefficients was less than~$10^{-10}$.
elements.sent-2411-387: Now that we have computed the wave-guide modes, we can solve~\eqref{eqn2.13.001 with~$u^{\In}$ equal to~$u_{j;l,+}$.}
elements.sent-2411-388: Using~\eqref{eq:proj_form , we can compute the guided-mode components of~$u^{\out}$ by \begin{equation}u^{\out}_g = \lp\sum_j c^l_{j,-}u_{j;l,-}\rp \chi_{x_1<0}+ \lp\sum_j c^r_{j,+}u_{j;r,+}\rp\chi_{x_1>0} .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-389: The difference~$u^{\out _c:=u^{\out -u^{\out _g$ represents the portion of the field that is radiated away from the junction.}}}
elements.sent-2411-390: The components~$u^{\out _g$ and~$u^{\out _c$ are shown in~\figref{fig:radiating_split .}}}
elements.sent-2411-391: This calculation shows that a much larger portion of the wave is transmitted than reflected.
elements.sent-2411-392: We can also see that a large portion is radiated away.
elements.sent-2411-393: This calculation represents the information in one column of the scattering matrix introduced in Section~\ref{ss.scat_matrix.003 .}
elements.sent-2411-394: With these projections, we are able to determine how much power is sent into each wave-guide and how much is lost.
elements.sent-2411-395: Using the formulas from Section~\ref{ss.scat_matrix.003 we see that the transmitted power is~$P_r:=\sum_j \xi_j^l |c^r_{j,+ |^2$.}}
elements.sent-2411-397: The total outgoing power is \begin{equation}P^{\out}= P_r+P_l+P_{\rad}:= \lim_{R\to\infty}\Im\int_{|x|=R} \overline{u^{\out}} \partial_{r} u^{\out} \,Rd\theta\label{eq:rad_p_def}\end{equation} $\bs \nu$ is the outward normal to~$\Gamma$.

elements.sent-2411-478: In this paper we have introduced the class of decomposable scattering problems on unbounded domains.
elements.sent-2411-479: We begin with an operator $L.$ A problem is decomposable if the computational domain can be split up into pieces, such that, in each unbounded component, the operator $L$ has an outgoing fundamental solution that can be computed efficiently.
elements.sent-2411-480: We show how to use the method of fundamental solutions to reduce the problem to systems of integral equations on the union of the domain boundaries.
elements.sent-2411-481: The method becomes practicable if the domain fundamental solutions have analytic continuations that allow the resulting integral equation to be solved using coordinate complexification and standard integral equations techniques.
elements.sent-2411-482: When implemented naively, these integral equation methods can be slow, as the evaluations of the kernel functions of the domain fundamental solutions require solving a PDE.
elements.sent-2411-483: We therefore introduce a method for building compressed representations of these domain fundamental solutions, which allow us to solve these integral equations efficiently.
elements.sent-2411-484: This method is applied to the case of 2 semi-infinite wave-guides meeting along a common perpendicular line.
elements.sent-2411-485: We conclude with a number of other examples of decomposable problems, including a wave-guide with a Y-junction, a wave-guide with a bend, and Dirichlet wave-guides.
elements.sent-2411-486: In future works, we plan to provide a rigorous justification for our use of coordinate complexification to solve~\eqref{eqn2.13.001 and to extend this method to other decomposable problems.. .}

elements.section-2411-010: Representation of Solutions Near to Channel Ends
elements.sent-2411-523: We show that the outgoing part of a solution to $(\Delta+q+k^2)u=0$ has a representation in a conic neighborhood of each channel end in terms of the bi-infinite wave-guide L.A.P.
elements.sent-2411-524: In this appendix we assume that the incoming part of $u$ comes from a wave-guide mode.
elements.sent-2411-525: Similar arguments will apply with other kinds of incoming data.
elements.sent-2411-526: We carry out this argument in $d$-dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-527: Let $v_l^j({\bx ')e^{-i\xi^j_lx_1}$ be an incoming wave guide mode associated to the $j$th channel, where we have chosen coordinates so that $\omega_j=(1,0,\dots,0).$ We choose a $\cC^{\infty}$ conic cut-off \begin{equation}\Psi_{\delta}^j(r\omega)=\varphi_+(r)\psi_{\delta}(\langle\omega,\omega_j\rangle),\end{equation} where $\varphi_+(r)=0,$ for $r<r_j$ and $1$ for $r>2r_j,$ and \begin{equation}\psi_{\delta}(t)= \begin{cases} 1\text{ for }t>1-\delta, 0\text{ for }t<<1-2\delta. \end{cases}\end{equation} Set $\tu_j({\bx )=\Psi_{\delta ^j({\bx )v_l^j({\bx ')e^{-i\xi^j_lx_1 , $ and observe that \begin{equation}w_j=(\Delta+q+k^2)\tu_j=2\nabla\Psi_{\delta}^j\cdot\nabla(v_l^j({\bx}')e^{-i\xi^j_lx_1})+ \Delta(\Psi_{\delta}^j)v_l^j({\bx}')e^{-i\xi^j_lx_1}\in\cS(\bbR^d).\end{equation}.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-528: This follows as the terms $\nabla\Psi_\delta^j$ and $\Delta \Psi_\delta^j$ are supported near a cone with axis along the positive $x_1$-axis, and $v_l^j({\bx ')$ decays exponentially in this set.}
elements.sent-2411-529: This shows that $w_j$ is in the domain of the L.A.P.
elements.sent-2411-530: By choosing $\delta'<\delta$ we can arrange to have $\supp\Psi^j_{\delta'}\subset\tV_j;$ as $\Psi^j_{\delta'}$ depends only on $\omega,$ for large enough $r,$ we see that \begin{equation}f_j=(\Delta+q_j+k^2)[\Psi^j_{\delta'}\tw_j]=2\nabla \Psi^j_{\delta'}\cdot\nabla\tw_j+(\Delta\Psi^j_{\delta'})\tw_j=O(|{\bx}|^{-\frac{d+3}{2}}),\end{equation} which shows that $f_j$ is in the domain of the $j$th L.A.P.
elements.sent-2411-531: But $\Psi^j_{\delta'}\tw_j$ is also an outgoing solution to this equation, and therefore $g_j=\Psi^j_{\delta'}\tw_j,$ which implies that \begin{equation}\begin{split} \Psi^j_{\delta'}\tw_j({\bx}) =\lim_{R\to\infty}\int_{B_R} R_j(k^2+i0)({\bx};{\by})(\Delta+q_j+k^2)\Psi^j_{\delta'}\tw_j({\by})d\by =\lim_{R\to\infty}\int_{\pa B_R} [R_j(k^2+i0)({\bx};{\by})\pa_r\Psi^j_{\delta'}\tw_j-\pa_r R_j(k^2+i0)({\bx};{\by})\Psi^j_{\delta'}\tw_j]dS_{\by} \phantom{mmmmmm}+\Psi^j_{\delta'}\tw_j({\bx}). \end{split}\end{equation} Therefore \begin{equation}\lim_{R\to\infty}\int_{\pa B_R} [R_j(k^2+i0)({\bx};{\by})\pa_r\Psi^j_{\delta'}\tw_j-\pa_r R_j(k^2+i0)({\bx};{\by})\Psi^j_{\delta'}\tw_j]dS_{\by}=0.\end{equation}.
